demoScenarios.name: Normal Pregnancy - Week 28
demoScenarios.description: Healthy pregnancy trajectory with low risk

conversationHistory.content: Any good nutrition tips for week 28?

At week 28, focus on iron-rich foods to support increased blood volume and prevent anemia. Good sources include leafy greens, lean meats, legumes, and fortified cereals. Pair them with vitamin C for better absorption. Well done on maintaining a healthy pregnancy journey!

demoScenarios.name: GDM Risk - Week 18
demoScenarios.description: Pregnancy with family history of gestational diabetes - moderate monitoring

conditions.name: gestational_diabetes
conditions.recommendation: Schedule glucose tolerance test. Monitor diet and exercise.

conversationHistory.content: I have a family history of diabetes. Should I be worried?

Family history means you have a higher risk for gestational diabetes, but it's very manageable! Your doctor will likely recommend a glucose tolerance test around week 24-28. In the meantime, maintaining a balanced diet with complex carbs, regular physical activity, and staying hydrated can help. You're being proactive, and that's excellent!

demoScenarios.name: Preeclampsia Risk - Week 35 (URGENT)
demoScenarios.description: High-risk pregnancy with preeclampsia symptoms requiring immediate attention

conditions.name: preeclampsia
conditions.recommendation: URGENT: Contact doctor immediately. Consider hospital monitoring.

conversationHistory.content: I have a severe headache and my feet are swollen. Should I go to the hospital?

⚠️ URGENT: Severe headaches combined with significant swelling can be signs of preeclampsia, especially given your medical history. This requires immediate medical evaluation. Please call your doctor right now or go to the emergency room. Do not delay. Your safety and your baby's safety are our priority.

demoScenarios.name: Postpartum Week 2 - Mental Health Screening
demoScenarios.description: Recent mother, postpartum depression screening, recovery tracking

conditions.name: postpartum_depression_risk
conditions.recommendation: Continue mood monitoring. Schedule mental health check-in with OB-GYN.

conversationHistory.content: I feel really exhausted and sometimes sad. Is this normal postpartum?

It's completely normal to feel tired after birth—your body is recovering from a major event. Some sadness in the first two weeks (baby blues) is common. However, if you're feeling persistently sad, hopeless, anxious, or having trouble bonding with baby, that's important to discuss with your doctor. These could be signs of postpartum depression, which is treatable. Please reach out to your healthcare provider.

demoScenarios.name: Family Support Mode - Partner View
demoScenarios.description: Partner/family member access to shared pregnancy dashboard

partnerTips: Accompany to appointments when possible—showing support strengthens the bond
Give gentle massages for back pain relief
Help with household tasks—pregnancy fatigue is real

conditions.name: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
conditions.simplifiedExplanation: A type of diabetes that develops during pregnancy when your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels.
conditions.whenToContact: If you have excessive thirst, frequent urination, or unusual fatigue
safetyTips: Choose complex carbs over simple sugars
Exercise 30 minutes daily (or as approved by doctor)

conditions.name: Preeclampsia
conditions.simplifiedExplanation: High blood pressure during pregnancy that can affect mother and baby health if not managed.
conditions.whenToContact: IMMEDIATELY if you experience severe headache, vision changes, or severe swelling

conditions.name: Postpartum Depression
conditions.simplifiedExplanation: A serious but treatable medical condition affecting mood after childbirth.
conditions.whenToContact: If you feel persistently sad, hopeless, or unable to care for your baby
safetyTips: Talk to your doctor or therapist
Ask for support from family and friends
Join support groups for new mothers
Be gentle with yourself—recovery takes time

conditions.name: Morning Sickness
conditions.simplifiedExplanation: Nausea and vomiting in early pregnancy, often triggered by hormonal changes.
conditions.whenToContact: If you can't keep any food or liquids down, or if vomiting is severe and frequent
safetyTips: Ginger tea or candies may help
Get fresh air and stay hydrated
